It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Maze navigation puzzle. Player is a black dot at (10,16) inside a blue 5x5 start region. Target is a blue region at bottom-right with a blue dot. White corridors are walkable paths, black (B) are walls. Red elements may be obstacles or path markers. Blue bar at row 63 is likely a HUD timer.
- Goal model: Navigate from start (top-left blue region) to target (bottom-right blue region) through white corridors.
- Action model: UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT move the player through the maze.
- Plan: First probe with RIGHT to confirm movement mechanics, then solve the maze path.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 80 actions; a strong score needs about 78 or fewer. You are 1.0x over the target.

- After RIGHT: [[20, 26], [20, 30], [24, 30], [24, 26]] (rows 20-24, cols 26-30)
- After DOWN: [[20, 26], [20, 30], [24, 30], [24, 26]] (no change)
- After RIGHT2: [[20, 26], [20, 30], [24, 30], [24, 26]] (no change)
- After DOWN2: [[20, 26], [20, 30], [24, 30], [24, 26]] (no change)

So the player is still at rows 20-24, cols 26-30. DOWN and RIGHT don't work from here.

From rows 20-24, cols 26-30, the player can move RIGHT to rows 20-24, cols 32-36 (into the white corridor). But the output shows that RIGHT from rows 20-24, cols 26-30 doesn't change the player's position.
Wait, the output shows:
- After RIGHT: [[20, 26], [20, 30], [24, 30], [24, 26]] (rows 20-24, cols 26-30)
This is the same as the starting position. So RIGHT doesn't work from rows 20-24, cols 26-30.

- Current player: [[20, 20], [20, 24], [24, 24], [24, 20]] (rows 20-24, cols 20-24)
- After RIGHT: [[20, 26], [20, 30], [24, 30], [24, 26]] (rows 20-24, cols 26-30)

- After DOWN: [[20, 26], [20, 30], [24, 30], [24, 26]] (no change)

- After RIGHT2: [[20, 26], [20, 30], [24, 30], [24, 26]] (no change)

- After DOWN2: [[20, 26], [20, 30], [24, 30], [24, 26]] (no change)
So DOWN from rows 20-24, cols 26-30 doesn't work.
So from rows 20-24, cols 26-30, neither RIGHT nor DOWN works. The player is stuck.
But wait, the player can move LEFT from rows 20-24, cols 26-30 back to rows 20-24, cols 20-24. And from rows 20-24, cols 20-24, the player can move RIGHT back to rows 20-24, cols 26-30.

Hmm, maybe the player needs to move DOWN from a different position. Let me try moving DOWN from rows 20-24, cols 20-24.

But looking at the image, the player appears to be in a blue region that's part of the white corridor. The white corridor is at rows 14-38, cols 32-38, but the player is at cols 26-30, which is to the left of the white corridor.
Wait, looking at the image, the player is in a blue region that's connected to the white corridor. The white corridor extends to the left at rows 20-24, cols 26-30.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: World model update: From rows 20-24, cols 26-30, RIGHT and DOWN are blocked. Player can only move LEFT/RIGHT between cols 20-24 and 26-30. Let me try DOWN from cols 20-24.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Player is at rows 20-24, cols 26-30. Move LEFT to cols 20-24, then try DOWN
action(['LEFT'], intent='solve')
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'b' and n['pixels'] == 24:
        print(f"After LEFT: {n['boundary']}")

# Try DOWN from cols 20-24
action(['DOWN'], intent='solve')
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'b' and n['pixels'] == 24:
        print(f"After DOWN: {n['boundary']}")

# Try RIGHT
action(['RIGHT'], intent='solve')
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'b' and n['pixels'] == 24:
        print(f"After RIGHT: {n['boundary']}")

# Try DOWN again
action(['DOWN'], intent='solve')
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'b' and n['pixels'] == 24:
        print(f"After DOWN2: {n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
